title: Steak au Poivre
subtitle: French pepper steak with brandy cream sauce
excerpt: Sirloin steaks crusted in coarsely cracked black pepper, seared hard in butter, then rested while a brandy-and-cream sauce is built in the same pan from the drippings. A great bistro dish.
sourceNotes: Steak au poivre is a classic French bistro dish, documented in Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, and Simone Beck, Mastering the Art of French Cooking (1961) and Elizabeth David, French Provincial Cooking (1960). The flambéing of the brandy is traditional but optional.

content.text: The pepper in steak au poivre must be coarsely cracked, not ground. Ground pepper disappears into the meat; cracked pepper forms a crust with discrete bursts of heat. Press the pepper firmly into the steak so it adheres, if it falls off in the pan, press it back in.

The sauce is made from the drippings, brandy, and cream. Flambéing the brandy is classic but carries risk, skip it if you'd prefer, and let the brandy reduce in the pan instead. The result is slightly different (more alcoholic bite) but equally correct.

items.name: Black pepper
items.prepNote: coarsely cracked, crush in a mortar or under a heavy pan

content.text: Season the steaks with salt. Spread the cracked pepper on a plate. Press both sides of each steak firmly into the pepper so it adheres in a thick crust. Melt 20 g of the butter in a cast-iron skillet over high heat until beginning to smoke. Sear the steaks for 2 to 3 minutes per side for medium-rare, or until your preferred doneness. Transfer to a warm plate and rest for 5 minutes.

Reduce the heat to medium. Melt the remaining 20 g butter in the pan and cook the shallots for 2 to 3 minutes until soft. Add the brandy (standing back, if flambéing, tilt the pan briefly to catch a flame, or simply let the brandy simmer for 30 seconds to cook off the alcohol). Add the cream and stir, scraping up any browned bits. Simmer for 2 to 3 minutes until slightly thickened. Taste and season. Pour over the rested steaks.

items.fix: Press very firmly, hold for 5 seconds on each side; the pepper needs the weight of your hand to stick
items.cause: Not pressed firmly enough into the meat

items.fix: Simmer for 1 to 2 minutes longer, swirling the pan; the sauce should coat the back of a spoon
items.cause: Not reduced enough after adding the cream

items.fix: Remove from the pan a degree or two below target temperature, resting on a warm plate finishes it
items.cause: Cast iron retains heat and continues to cook the steak off the hob

content.text: Steak au poivre is the classic Parisian brasserie steak, on every menu, ordered by everyone, made seriously only in the places that care. The brandy flambé at the table was a standard restaurant performance from the 1950s to the 1980s; now it is usually done in the kitchen. The dish rewards a good cut and proper resting more than any technique in the sauce.
